stories.headline: Metro Denver housing market surges in February as mortgage rates dip below 6%
stories.summary:
Metro Denver's housing market posted its strongest monthly gains in years during February, with home and condo sales jumping nearly 30% from January to 2,629 closings, according to the Denver Metro Association of Realtors. Pending sales rose 29.2% on the month to 3,737 and are running 15.3% above February 2025 levels, signaling continued momentum heading into spring.

The activity was driven in part by 30-year mortgage rates briefly falling below 6% in February — a psychologically significant threshold — giving buyers more purchasing power after years of elevated rates. New listings climbed 12.1% and inventory rose a near-record 9.2%, reaching 8,988 available homes and condos as of Feb. 28.

The median sales price for single-family homes rose 2.4% from January to $630,000, though it remains 2.2% below year-ago levels. Condos and townhomes continued to soften, with median prices falling 2.82% from January and 5.25% year-over-year to $379,000, a trend partly attributed to rising HOA fees driven by higher insurance premiums.

DMAR Market Trends Committee Chair Amanda Snitker noted that multiple offers have returned for well-priced, move-in-ready properties in desirable locations. She cautioned that mortgage rates have since ticked back up to 6.13%, and a sustained conflict with Iran keeping oil prices elevated could threaten further rate declines.

stories.headline: Colorado lawmakers fast-track bills expanding ballot access timelines, banning dual officeholding
stories.summary:
Colorado legislators are advancing two election-related bills on expedited timelines ahead of the 2026 election cycle. House Bill 1113, which passed the House on a party-line vote, would extend the window for ballot drop boxes from 15 to 22 days before an election and mail ballots to voters 29 days before primary and general elections, up from 22 days currently.

The bill's sponsors say the expanded timelines are a response to U.S. Postal Service rule changes backed by the Trump administration that will eliminate same-day postmarking of mail, potentially delaying ballots sent close to election deadlines. Though Colorado does not require ballots to be postmarked by Election Day — they must be in clerks' hands by 7 p.m. on Election Day — voting rights groups argue the changes create confusion and urgency for mail voters.

A second measure moving through the legislature would bar elected officials from simultaneously holding multiple elected offices, a provision sponsors describe as a conflict-of-interest safeguard. Both bills cleared their first chambers this week.

Critics, including opponents who submitted form emails to committee members, argued HB 1113 goes beyond technical cleanup and makes sweeping changes to election systems, including expanding drop-box access and weakening voter challenge mechanisms. Co-sponsor Rep. Emily Sirota, D-Denver, said the bill's provisions largely originated from county clerks.

stories.headline: Gov. Polis signals he may grant clemency to convicted election clerk Tina Peters
stories.summary:
Colorado Gov. Jared Polis has given what analysts describe as the clearest indication yet that he is considering clemency for Tina Peters, the former Mesa County clerk convicted of crimes related to a 2021 breach of election equipment. Peters has been a prominent figure among supporters of former President Donald Trump's claims of election fraud.

Polis, a Democrat, drew attention after posting on X comparing Peters' case to other clemency considerations, prompting speculation that he may act before she begins serving her sentence. Peters was sentenced in 2024 to nine years in prison following her conviction on multiple felony and misdemeanor counts.

The potential clemency decision would be politically significant, pitting Polis's stated principles on government overreach and individual liberty against the concerns of election security advocates who view Peters' prosecution as a necessary accountability measure. Polis has at times positioned himself as a libertarian-leaning Democrat willing to cross partisan lines on certain criminal justice matters.

stories.headline: Colorado legislature considers bill that would redefine first-degree murder, require at least two deaths
stories.summary:
A bill introduced in the Colorado legislature, House Bill 26-1281, would significantly narrow the definition of first-degree murder under a provision covering extreme indifference to human life. Under the proposal, a perpetrator would need to cause the deaths of at least two people to qualify for a first-degree murder charge under that standard; killing a single person would be reclassified as second-degree murder.

The bill includes limited exceptions: killing a law enforcement officer or a child under 12 could still constitute first-degree murder, as could killing one person while also causing serious bodily injury to two or more others with a deadly weapon. Sponsors framed the bill as addressing what they called confusion and imbalance in Colorado statutes covering extreme indifference homicides.

Critics, including the Denver Gazette editorial board and veteran district attorneys, have condemned the measure as a dangerous softening of criminal statutes that would, among other scenarios, prevent a hired killer from facing first-degree murder charges for a single contract killing. The bill has been introduced by Democratic members of the legislature.

The proposal is the latest in a series of criminal justice reform measures that have divided Colorado lawmakers and drawn sharp rebukes from prosecutors who argue that years of legislative changes have weakened the state's ability to hold violent offenders accountable.

stories.headline: Colorado Supreme Court orders Children's Hospital Colorado to explain why it halted transgender care services
stories.summary:
The Colorado Supreme Court issued an order Tuesday directing Children's Hospital Colorado to explain why the court should not compel it to resume providing certain services to transgender children, citing the state's anti-discrimination law. The order follows a Denver court ruling last month that had addressed the hospital's decision to halt some gender-affirming care.

Children's Hospital Colorado had scaled back or paused certain transgender-related medical services, a move that drew legal challenges from advocates who argued the hospital's actions violated state non-discrimination protections. Colorado law prohibits discrimination in public accommodations, including healthcare facilities, on the basis of gender identity.

The Supreme Court's order requiring the hospital to justify its position represents a significant escalation of the legal dispute and could set a statewide precedent for how Colorado healthcare institutions must respond to non-discrimination obligations in the context of gender-affirming care.

stories.headline: Avalanche face Anaheim in final game before NHL trade deadline, carry 40-10-9 record
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche will close out a back-to-back road trip Wednesday night against the Anaheim Ducks at 8:08 p.m. MT, their final contest before the NHL trade deadline. Colorado enters the game with a 40-10-9 record, maintaining one of the best marks in the league and a commanding lead in the Central Division.

The projected lineup features Nathan MacKinnon centered between Gabriel Landeskog and Martin Necas on the top line, with Brock Nelson at center on the second unit alongside Ross Colton and Valeri Nichushkin. On defense, Devon Toews is paired with Cale Makar, while veteran Brent Burns skates alongside Josh Manson.

The game carries added significance as it precedes the trade deadline, with the organization expected to evaluate whether to add depth pieces for a playoff run. Goaltender Mackenzie Blackwood is listed as the starter against Anaheim.

stories.headline: Rockies prospect Roldy Brito named organization's 2025 Minor League Player of the Year
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rockies announced Monday that 18-year-old infielder and outfielder Roldy Brito has been named the 2025 Doug Million Minor League Player of the Year, the organization's top prospect honor. Brito, a native of Bonao, Dominican Republic, was dominant at the Arizona Complex League level, slashing .368/.445/.555 with 46 runs, 22 stolen bases and a 1.000 OPS in 51 games before finishing the season at Single-A Fresno.

Brito earned ACL Most Valuable Player honors and was named an ACL Post-Season All-Star and a Baseball America Rookie All-Star. He ranked first in the ACL in batting average and finished in the top three in OPS, hits, on-base percentage, slugging and total bases. Baseball America rates him as the Rockies' No. 5 prospect.

Left-handed pitcher Konner Eaton was named the Rick Matthews Minor League Pitcher of the Year after recording 149 strikeouts — the most by any Rockies minor leaguer — in 27 starts split between High-A Spokane and Double-A Hartford, posting a 3.79 ERA. Physical Performance Manager Trevor Swartz, a 12-year member of the organization, received the P.J. Carey Player Development Award.

stories.headline: Broncos place second-round tender on cornerback Ja'Quan McMillian, decline to tender Jaleel McLaughlin
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos are making decisions on their restricted free agents as the start of NFL free agency approaches. The team has placed a second-round tender on cornerback Ja'Quan McMillian, worth nearly $6 million, securing his rights for the 2026 season while allowing other teams to make an offer that Denver could match or receive a second-round pick in compensation.

The Broncos have opted not to tender running back Jaleel McLaughlin, meaning he will become an unrestricted free agent. McLaughlin served as a depth contributor in Denver's backfield. The team also met with several running backs at the NFL Scouting Combine, with pass protection noted as a key area of focus in those discussions.

Denver is actively seeking to add offensive weapons to support second-year quarterback Bo Nix, whose team finished 14th in scoring last season. Reports indicate mutual interest between the Broncos and New York Jets running back Breece Hall should he become available in free agency.

stories.headline: Cold front brings rain to Denver Tuesday; Fire Weather Watch issued Thursday, winter storm possible late week
stories.summary:
A cold front moved into the Denver metro area Tuesday, dropping temperatures roughly 20 degrees from Monday's near-record high of 70 degrees to the upper 40s. Scattered showers moved across the Front Range Tuesday, with brief snow possible along the Palmer Divide and in the foothills overnight into early Wednesday. Mountain areas saw snow throughout the day, though accumulations were limited to a few inches.

A Fire Weather Watch has been issued for areas south of Interstate 70 from noon to 6 p.m. Thursday, with forecasters citing wind gusts up to 45 mph, humidity values of 10 to 15 percent and unseasonably warm temperatures near 70 degrees. Conditions are expected to clear Wednesday before another warm-up Thursday.

A more significant winter storm system is expected to arrive late Thursday night into Friday, bringing heavy mountain snow and a potential rain and snow mix to the I-25 corridor. Forecasters say measurable snowfall in the Denver metro is looking increasingly likely, though specific amounts are still being refined. Warmer and drier weather is forecast for the weekend.

stories.headline: Colorado Parks and Wildlife opens big game hunting license application period through April 7
stories.summary:
Colorado Parks and Wildlife has begun its primary big game hunting license application period for 2026, running through April 7. Hunters seeking limited-by-quota tags for deer, elk, pronghorn, moose and bear can apply online at cpw.state.co.us/buy-apply or by phone. A qualifying small game and fishing license and a habitat stamp are required to enter the draw.

New for 2026, hunters will pay an additional $1.25 fee on licenses to help fund search and rescue teams. The season also brings new chronic wasting disease testing requirements: hunters who harvest elk during rifle seasons in certain designated areas must submit free CWD tests, though deer testing will not be required this year.

In a notable development, bison now appears in the big game brochure following 2025 legislation that reclassified the animals as big game in Colorado, a move intended to protect wild bison migrating into the state from Utah. CPW is not proposing regular bison hunting seasons at this time but is developing a Book Cliffs Bison Herd Management Plan and has opened a roster for potential director-approved special management licenses. A secondary draw period will run June 18-30 for remaining tags.

stories.headline: Delta Air Lines unveils expanded Sky Club lounge at Denver International Airport
stories.summary:
Delta Air Lines has announced the opening of a renovated and expanded Sky Club lounge at Denver International Airport, adding capacity at one of its busiest hubs. The expansion is part of Delta's broader effort to upgrade airport lounge facilities for its frequent flyers and premium cabin passengers at high-traffic airports.

Denver International Airport is among the nation's busiest airports, and the Sky Club expansion reflects continued investment in passenger amenities at DIA as competition among carriers for premium travelers intensifies. Delta has been upgrading Sky Club facilities at key hubs nationwide following increased demand and past overcrowding complaints.

stories.headline: Denver PrideFest relocates to downtown core for summer 2026 celebration
stories.summary:
Denver PrideFest, one of the region's largest annual LGBTQ+ events, is moving into the heart of downtown Denver for its 2026 edition, organizers announced. The event has historically been centered near Civic Center Park and along Colfax Avenue, drawing hundreds of thousands of attendees each year.

The relocation into the downtown core is intended to increase accessibility and expand the event's footprint, allowing for a larger celebration and broader participation from businesses and community organizations. Specific dates and street closures have not yet been publicly detailed.

weather.notable: Nearly 5 degrees above the average high for this time of year.

joke.joke: I don't know why people say 'grow some backbone.' I have a backbone. It's just a little more flexible than most.

topStory.headline: Senate to Vote Wednesday on War Powers Resolution Seeking to Restrain Trump's Military Campaign Against Iran
topStory.summary:
The U.S. Senate is set to vote Wednesday on a war powers resolution that would require congressional authorization before President Donald Trump can continue military strikes against Iran. The measure, led by Democratic Sen. Tim Kaine of Virginia and co-sponsored by Republican Sen. Rand Paul of Kentucky, would direct the removal of U.S. armed forces from hostilities against Iran not authorized by Congress. The vote comes five days after Trump launched a surprise military attack on Iran last Saturday without seeking congressional approval.

The resolution faces steep odds in the Republican-controlled Senate, where Democrats hold 47 seats and need a simple majority to advance the measure. Democratic Sen. John Fetterman of Pennsylvania has pledged to vote against the resolution, meaning it would require at least four Republican votes beyond Paul's to pass. Even if it cleared both chambers, Trump could veto the measure, and Democrats lack the two-thirds majorities in both houses needed to override.

Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer said Tuesday that the operation lacks clear objectives and called it a war of choice. Democratic Sens. Chris Murphy of Connecticut and Cory Booker of New Jersey said administration officials acknowledged in a classified briefing that the operation is open-ended, that more American casualties are expected, and that ground troops have not been ruled out. Secretary of State Marco Rubio, speaking at the Capitol Tuesday, said the administration does not intend to put American troops in harm's way, though six U.S. military members were killed over the weekend in a drone attack. Trump has said the operation is projected to last four to five weeks but indicated the U.S. has the capability to sustain it longer.

The conflict is already triggering wider regional escalation. Israel launched a new ground incursion into southern Lebanon on Tuesday and conducted consecutive days of airstrikes on Beirut's southern suburbs, targeting what it described as Hezbollah command centers and weapons storage facilities. A senior Hezbollah official responded that the group had no option but to return to resistance, declaring that the era of patience had ended. The Lebanese army withdrew from at least seven forward operating positions along the Israeli border amid the intensifying strikes.

Kaine, who led a similar war powers effort in June that failed, said the vote serves the purpose of forcing lawmakers onto the record on a war that directly affects U.S. military personnel. The Senate and House votes represent the most significant congressional challenge yet to Trump's authority over the Iran campaign, and their outcomes will signal how much latitude Republicans are willing to grant the administration as the conflict expands with no defined exit strategy.
